item.title: Forecasting intermittent demand: why our MAPE was lying to us
item.description:
Problem

Around 60% of the SKUs in our fastener catalogue sell fewer than twelve times a year. When we evaluated candidate models on MAPE, the “winner” was consistently the one that forecast close to zero for these items — which is exactly the forecast that causes a stockout the one week a mining customer orders four thousand M14 locknuts.

The metric wasn’t measuring what the business cared about. Before comparing any more models, we had to fix the yardstick.

item.title: Regex first, LLM fallback: a document extraction pattern that cut our API cost 92%
item.description:
Problem

Freight forwarder and supplier emails carry a dozen reference types — PO numbers, invoice numbers, container numbers, HAWB, vessel names. Sending every email to an LLM works, but at volume the cost and latency are hard to justify for fields that follow rigid formats.

Background

Most reference types are near-regular languages. A container number is four letters and seven digits with a check digit; an Australian PO in our system is a fixed prefix plus six digits. The hard residue is the unstructured 10–15% — references buried in prose, typos, or scanned attachments.

item.title: Safety stock is a policy decision, not a formula
item.description:
Problem

The textbook safety stock formula assumes you have already decided your service level. In practice that decision — how often are we willing to stock out, per SKU class, and who signs off on it — is the entire problem.

Background

Demand variability and lead time variability are inputs. Service level is a trade-off between working capital and customer promises, which makes it a management policy, not a statistical parameter.

item.title: About
item.description:
I’m Norman Li, a supply chain professional in Sydney working across demand planning, procurement, and business analysis — currently in industrial fasteners serving mining, defence, and structural steel markets.

My day-to-day spans supplier management across the UK, Europe, China and the US, RFQ engineering, and building the automation that makes procurement document processing bearable: Python pipelines, Power Automate flows, and increasingly LLM-based extraction with deterministic guardrails.

Stack

Python (pandas, scikit-learn, LightGBM), SQL, Power BI, Power Query, Power Automate, Excel at the level where it becomes a liability, and the Anthropic API for document workflows.
